steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for the main idea of the passage. Morris tells an anecdote about a rough Channel crossing — but notice that who else stayed well keeps changing: first the captain, then the second mate, then 'one other man.' Then possibly no one else stayed well. The one constant is that Morris himself is always the healthy one.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Morris is worried that the captain will start to feel sick during this boat trip.: The setup mentions a current boat trip, but nothing indicates that Morris is worried about the captain feeling sick during it. ✗
  • Choice B — Morris has a hard time remembering his first trip across the English Channel when his friends ask about it.: No friends ask him anything in the passage; the shifting details look deliberate, not like a memory lapse. ✗
  • Choice C — When Morris speaks of an earlier trip, he often changes the details but always brags about his own wellness.: Exactly what the passage shows — the companion changes every time, yet Morris is always well. ✓
  • Choice D — Morris spent most of his time with a particular passenger during a previous boat trip.: The passage never identifies a single passenger; the companion keeps changing. ✗
C is correct.
